## Topic

The project needs authoritative literature for the chart-algebra piece (ii) closure on `AlgebraicJacobian/Cotangent/ChartAlgebra.lean` + `ChartAlgebraS3.lean`. Specifically, the four (S3.*) sub-claims in the path-(b) decomposition of `constants_integral_over_base_field`:

1. **(S3.sep.1)** `Smooth ⇒ IsGeometricallyReduced` on Γ — Stacks Project Tag 04QM (smooth implies geometrically reduced for morphisms of schemes), Tag 0334 (definition of geometrically reduced).
2. **(S3.sep.2)** `IsGeometricallyReduced + FiniteDimensional ⇒ IsSeparable` for field extensions — Stacks Project Tag 0BJF (and surrounding Tag 030V, 030W, 030X on separable / geometrically reduced field extensions).
3. **(S3.pi.1)** Flat base change of Γ for proper schemes: the canonical map `Γ(X, O_X) ⊗_k K → Γ(X_K, O_{X_K})` is an isomorphism when X/k is proper and K/k is a field extension. Stacks Project Tag 02KH (cohomology and flat base change, specialised to H^0). Companion content: Tag 0BUG (base change for proper morphisms).
4. **(S3.pi.2)** `IsPurelyInseparable` from a unique-minimal-prime-of-base-change hypothesis — Stacks Project Tag 05DH (purely inseparable extensions), Tag 030V (purely inseparable closures).

Additionally for the KDM (Kähler differential module) bridge:

5. **(BR.2)–(BR.5)** standard-smooth ⇒ free Ω; coefficient derivations; `Differential.ContainConstants`. References: Stacks Project Tag 07F4 (universal derivations on standard-smooth algebras), Tag 07F1 (the cotangent module for standard smooth).

And for the broader project context (per user-hint: Hartshorne / Eisenbud / FGA / Matsumura):

6. **Hartshorne, "Algebraic Geometry"** — Chapter II §8 (Differentials, including II.8.6A, II.8.7), Chapter III §1 (Derived Functors), Chapter III §5 (cohomology of projective space), Chapter IV §1 (Riemann–Roch on curves; smooth curves over k = k̄).
7. **Eisenbud, "Commutative Algebra with a View Toward Algebraic Geometry"** — §17 (Differentials, especially 17.7 the Jacobian criterion), §16 (modules of differentials).
8. **Matsumura, "Commutative Ring Theory"** — §26 (Derivations), §28 (Separable extensions).
9. **FGA — Bourbaki Seminar 232** (Grothendieck, "Technique de descente. II. Le théorème d'existence en géométrie algébrique", Sém. Bourbaki 232, 1961/62; predecessor of FGA Explained Ch. 5 by Nitsure on Picard schemes). Also Bourbaki Seminar 221 (Grothendieck, "Technique de descente. I"). These are Route A (FGA Picard) references; needed for STRATEGY.md grounding even though Route A is off-critical-path.

39	
40	# Reference Retriever
41	
42	You fetch papers, books, or online mathematical content from the web and produce structured markdown summaries under the project's `references/` directory. You exist so that the plan agent and blueprint-writers have authoritative source material to draw on, rather than relying on the dispatcher's memory or guessing.
43	
44	## Your Job
45	
46	Your directive names one or more topics, mathematical questions, or specific sources (by arXiv ID, DOI, title + author, or URL). For each, you:
47	
48	1. Locate the source on the web (`WebSearch` + `WebFetch`).
49	2. Read the relevant portions — abstract, introduction, the section(s) most relevant to the directive's question.
50	3. Produce a structured markdown summary under `references/<slug>.md`.
51	4. Register the new entry in `references/summary.md`.
52	5. Report back to the dispatcher with a one-line status + the report path.
53	
54	You do **not** hallucinate citations or content. If a source is paywalled, retracted, or impossible to access, you report that honestly. The dispatcher prefers an honest "not found" over fabricated material every time.
55	
56	## Directive Format
57	
58	```markdown
59	# Reference Retriever Directive
60	
61	## Slug
62	<slug>
63	
64	## Topic
65	<one or two sentences naming the mathematical area + the specific question>
66	
67	## What the dispatcher will use this for
68	<one paragraph: which chapter / strategy decision the source will inform, so I can tailor the depth and angle of the summary>
69	
70	## Seeds (optional)
71	- arXiv: 1234.5678
72	- DOI: 10.xxxx/yyyy
73	- Title + author: "Some Paper" by Smith, year
74	- URL: https://...
75	- Search query: "<terms the dispatcher recommends>"
76	
77	(If no seeds are given, I search for the topic on arXiv + Google Scholar + Stacks Project + nLab as appropriate to the area.)
78	
79	## Out of scope
80	<sources or angles the dispatcher does NOT want — e.g. "no philosophy-of-math sources", "skip historical surveys, want technical content only">
81	
82	## Depth expected
83	- shallow: abstract + intro summary, one paragraph
84	- medium: section-by-section summary of the relevant chapters, ~1 page
85	- deep: full proof reconstruction of the key results, ~2-3 pages
86	```
87	
88	If the directive omits "Depth expected", default to **medium**.
89	
90	## What you do
91	
92	1. **Read your directive completely.**
93	
94	2. **Locate the source.**
95	   - If seeds are given, fetch them first (`WebFetch <url>`).
96	   - Otherwise, `WebSearch` with focused queries derived from the topic. Prefer:
97	     - arXiv for recent technical papers.
98	     - Stacks Project (https://stacks.math.columbia.edu) for algebraic geometry.
99	     - nLab (https://ncatlab.org) for category-theoretic content.
100	     - Online textbooks (Hartshorne, Vakil, Mumford's Red Book, etc.) when the topic is foundational.
101	   - If a seed is broken or paywalled, search for an open-access preprint or mirror.
102	
103	3. **Read enough of the source to write a faithful summary.** Abstract + introduction at minimum; the specific section(s) the directive points at if any are named.
104	
105	4. **Write the summary file** to `references/<slug>.md`. Format below.
106	
107	5. **Register the entry** in `references/summary.md`. Append (do not delete existing entries) a one-line index entry referencing your new file.
108	
109	6. **Verify the file is on disk** (run `ls references/<slug>.md`) before reporting back.
110	
111	## Summary file format
112	
113	```markdown
114	# <Title>
115	
116	## Citation
117	<author(s)>, "<title>", <venue / publisher>, <year>. <DOI / arXiv ID / URL>.
118	
119	## Slug
120	<slug>
121	
122	## Source URL(s)
123	- <primary URL>
124	- <mirror or alternative URL if any>
125	
126	## Why this source
127	
128	<2-3 sentences: which Archon chapter / strategy decision will use this. Copy from the directive's "What the dispatcher will use this for" if it captured it well.>
129	
130	## Abstract / Overview
131	
132	<1-2 paragraphs distilling the source's claim and scope.>
133	
134	## Relevant content (per the directive)
135	
136	<The section-by-section or theorem-by-theorem distillation of the parts the dispatcher will actually use. Use mathematical notation, name definitions and theorems explicitly. Cite page numbers / section numbers from the source where useful.>
137	
138	### Definitions
139	- **<name>** (<source, page/section>): <definition + mathematical notation>
140	- ...
141	
142	### Theorems / Lemmas
143	- **<name>** (<source, page/section>): statement. Proof sketch: <how the source proves it, summarized>. Prerequisites: <list>.
144	- ...
145	
146	### Cross-references the source relies on
147	<other papers / books the source cites for prerequisites that the dispatcher might need to retrieve separately. List them; do not retrieve unless the directive asked.>
148	
149	## Caveats
150	
151	<things the dispatcher should know about this source: paywalled, retracted, contested, uses unusual conventions, etc. If none, write "None".>
152	
153	## Quality assessment
154	
155	<one or two sentences: how reliable is this source for the dispatcher's use case? Is it the definitive reference for the topic, or one of several views? Are there known errata?>
156	```
157	
158	## `references/summary.md` registration
159	
160	Append one bullet to the existing list:
161	
162	```markdown
163	- [`<slug>.md`](./<slug>.md) — <one-line topic summary>
164	```
165	
166	If `references/summary.md` doesn't exist, create it with a minimal header before appending.
167	
168	## Rules
169	
170	### What you CAN do
171	- Use `WebSearch` and `WebFetch` to locate and read sources.
172	- Write to `references/<slug>.md` (your assigned filename).
173	- Append to `references/summary.md`.
174	
175	### What you MUST do
176	- **Verify before writing.** Do not summarize a source you couldn't actually fetch or read. If `WebFetch` returned an error or a paywall page, that source is unavailable — say so.
177	- **Cite exact section / page numbers** when possible. Vague "the paper discusses X" sentences are useless to the dispatcher.
178	- **Use mathematical notation, not Lean syntax.** This is for blueprint writers; they want math.
179	- **Preserve the source's conventions** when summarizing. If Hartshorne uses one convention and the dispatcher's project uses another, note the discrepancy in "Caveats" rather than silently translating.
180	
181	### What you MUST NOT do
182	- **Do NOT fabricate.** Never write content that wasn't in the source. "I believe the paper proves..." is not acceptable; you either verified it or you didn't.
183	- **Do NOT delete or rewrite existing `references/` entries.** Only add new ones. If an existing summary is wrong, report it in your own report's "Notes for Dispatcher" section.
184	- **Do NOT write outside `references/`.** Your write-domain is enforced.
185	- **Do NOT spawn child subagents.** I am a leaf.
186	
187	## Report format
188	
189	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/reference-retriever-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
190	
191	```markdown
192	# Reference Retriever Report
193	
194	## Slug
195	<slug>
196	
197	## Status
198	<COMPLETE | NOT_FOUND | PARTIAL>
199	<NOT_FOUND: source could not be located or accessed.
200	PARTIAL: some seeds resolved, others didn't.
201	COMPLETE: all directive items resolved.>
202	
203	## Sources fetched
204	
205	For each: title, URL, fetch status, summary file written.
206	
207	- "Some Paper" — https://arxiv.org/abs/1234.5678 — fetched OK — `references/some-paper.md`.
208	- "Other Book Ch. 5" — https://example.com/book — paywalled — NOT fetched, no summary written.
209	
210	## Index updates
211	- `references/summary.md` — appended <N> entries: `some-paper.md`, ...
212	
213	## Notes for Dispatcher
214	<- any source the directive named that turned out to be wrong or not where it was claimed to be
215	- any related source you noticed that the dispatcher might want next
216	- any pre-existing references/<slug>.md you noticed was wrong or stale (DO NOT modify — report only)>
217	```
218	
219	## Return value
220	
221	Your final assistant message:
222	
223	- One line: `<slug>: <status> — <N> sources fetched, <M> summary files written`
224	- The path to your full report.

Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and clears the file after the plan phase succeeds. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

# Section 33.25: Schemes Smooth over Fields

**Location:** Part 2 (Schemes), Chapter 33 (Varieties), Section 33.25

**Overview:** This section contains 11 lemmas characterizing smooth schemes over fields and their geometric properties.

---

## Key Lemmas

**Lemma 33.25.1** (Characteristic Zero)
- **Hypothesis:** $k$ is a field; $X$ is locally finite type over $k$; $\Omega_{X/k}$ is locally free; characteristic of $k$ is zero
- **Conclusion:** The structure morphism $X \to \text{Spec}(k)$ is smooth
- **Proof:** References Algebra, Lemma 10.140.7

**Lemma 33.25.2** (Perfect Field)
- **Hypothesis:** $k$ is a field; $X$ locally finite type over $k$; $\Omega_{X/k}$ locally free; $X$ reduced; $k$ perfect
- **Conclusion:** The structure morphism $X \to \text{Spec}(k)$ is smooth

**Lemma 33.25.3**
- **Conclusion:** "a smooth morphism $X \to \text{Spec}(k)$ (where $k$ is a field) yields $X$ a regular scheme"

**Lemma 33.25.8** (Perfect Field Characterization)
- For perfect field $k$ and reduced locally algebraic $k$-scheme $X$, the smooth locus equals the regular locus, forming a dense open subscheme

**Lemma 33.25.11** (Stratification)
- **Conclusion:** Any finite-type scheme over a field admits a finite purely inseparable extension and stratification into smooth locally closed pieces

# Proposition 10.162.15 (Nagata)

**Location:** The Stacks Project, Part 1 (Preliminaries), Chapter 10 (Commutative Algebra), Section 10.162 (Nagata rings)

## Statement

For a ring $R$, the following conditions are equivalent:

1. $R$ is a Nagata ring
2. Any finite type $R$-algebra is Nagata
3. $R$ is universally Japanese and Noetherian

## Proof Outline

The proof proceeds through five main steps:

**Step 1:** Reduce to the case where $S \cong R[x]/I$ by considering finitely generated extensions.

**Step 2:** Further reduce to proving that if $R$ is a Nagata domain with a monogenic extension $R \subset S$ of domains, then $S$ is N-2.

**Step 3:** Handle the purely transcendental case ($S = R[x]$) using referenced lemmas.

**Step 4:** For finite extensions of fraction fields, reduce to the case of normal Nagata domains.

**Step 5:** Complete the argument by showing that $S_{\mathfrak{m}}$ is analytically unramified, hence N-1, by verifying conditions of "Lemma 10.162.12 applies to the local ring $S_{\mathfrak{m}}$ and the element $x$."

## Key Referenced Tags

- Lemma 10.161.13, 10.161.15, 10.161.7
- Lemma 10.162.5, 10.162.6, 10.162.10, 10.162.12, 10.162.13
- Theorem 10.34.1

# Lemma 49.3.1 (Tag 0BJF) — The Stacks Project

**Location:** Part 3 (Topics in Scheme Theory), Chapter 49 (Discriminants and Differents), Section 49.3 (Discriminant of a finite locally free morphism)

## Statement

Let π : X → Y be a morphism of schemes which is finite locally free. Then π is étale if and only if its discriminant is empty.

## Proof Outline

The proof proceeds by reducing to a base case about finite-dimensional algebras:

1. **Reduction step:** By Morphisms, Lemma 29.36.8, it suffices to check the fibers of π are étale. Base change compatibility allows reduction to: given a field k and finite-dimensional k-algebra A, show A is étale over k iff the trace pairing Q_{A/k}(a,b) = Trace_{A/k}(ab) is nondegenerate.

2. **Forward direction (nondegeneracy implies étale):** If Q_{A/k} is nondegenerate, then:
   - Any nilpotent element a satisfies Q_{A/k}(a, −) ≡ 0, so A is reduced
   - A decomposes as a product of fields K₁ × ... × Kₙ
   - The trace pairing becomes an orthogonal direct sum of field pairings
   - Fields, Lemma 9.20.7 ensures each K_i is separable over k
   - Therefore A is étale by Algebra, Lemma 10.143.4

3. **Converse:** Proved by reversing the argument.

## Referenced Prerequisites
- Morphisms, Lemma 29.36.8
- Algebra, Lemmas 10.53.2, 10.53.6, 10.25.1, 10.143.4
- Fields, Lemma 9.20.7

# Lemma 30.5.2: Flat Base Change

**Tag:** 02KH

**Title:** Flat base change

## Statement

Consider a cartesian diagram of schemes:
```
X' --g'--> X
|           |
f'          f
|           |
v           v
S' --g----> S
```

Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module with pullback $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$. Assume $g$ is flat and $f$ is quasi-compact and quasi-separated.

**Conclusion:** For any $i \geq 0$:

1. The base change map is an isomorphism: "$g^*R^if_*\mathcal{F} \longrightarrow R^if'_*\mathcal{F}'$"

2. If $S = \text{Spec}(A)$ and $S' = \text{Spec}(B)$, then "$H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$"

## Proof Outline

The proof reduces part (1) to part (2) by localizing to affine base schemes. For part (2):

- **Separated case:** Uses Čech cohomology with affine covers, establishing that "$\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) = \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B$" and exploiting flatness of $A \to B$.

- **Quasi-separated case:** Applies the Čech-to-cohomology spectral sequence to compare the two situations via flat base change.

## Key Prerequisites

- Cohomology, Lemma 20.17.1 (base change map)
- Morphisms, Lemma 29.25.8 (base change preserves flatness)
- Lemma 30.5.1 (Čech complex base change)
- Lemma 30.4.5 and 30.4.6 (quasi-coherence of higher direct images)

# Lemma 33.9.3 (Tag 0BUG)

**Title:** Properties of global sections of structure sheaf on proper schemes

**Hypothesis:** Let $k$ be a field and $X$ a proper scheme over $k$.

**Main Conclusion:** The algebra $A = H^0(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ satisfies eight related properties, including:

- "A is a finite dimensional k-algebra"
- "$A$ is a product of Artinian local $k$-algebras, one factor for each connected component"
- If $X$ is reduced: "$A$ is a product of fields, each a finite extension of $k$"
- If $X$ is geometrically integral: "$A = k$"

**Proof Strategy:**

The proof proceeds inductively through the eight parts:

1. Finiteness follows from Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma 30.19.2
2. Local Artinian structure via Algebra Lemmas; idempotents correspond to connected components
3. Reducedness of $X$ implies $A$ is reduced, hence local factors are fields
4. Geometric reducedness implies separability using tensor products with algebraic closure
5. Geometric connectedness makes $A \otimes_k \overline{k}$ a zero-dimensional local ring (irreducible)
6. Follows from part (5)
7. Geometric connectedness + reducedness forces $A = k$
8. Follows from part (7)

# Lemma 38.7.4 (Tag 05DH) - Stacks Project

**Location:** Part 2 (Schemes), Chapter 38 (More on Flatness), Section 38.7 (Localization and universally injective maps)

## Statement

Given ring maps R → S and S → S', with N an S-module, assume:

1. "R → S is of finite presentation and N is of finite presentation over S"
2. "N is flat over R"
3. "S → S' is flat"
4. The image of Spec(S') → Spec(S) contains all primes q where q is an associated prime of N ⊗_R κ(p) (p being the inverse image of q in R)

**Conclusion:** "N → N ⊗_S S' is R-universally injective"

## Proof Outline

The proof reduces to localization: By Algebra Lemma 10.82.11, it suffices to verify that "N_q → (N ⊗_R S')_q is R_p-universally injective for any prime q of S lying over p in R." The proof then applies Lemma 38.7.3 to localized ring maps and modules.

## Prerequisites

- Algebra, Lemma 10.82.11
- Lemma 38.7.3 (previous section)

# Lemma 10.44.4 Statement and Context

**Chapter:** Chapter 10: Commutative Algebra, Section 10.44: Separable extensions, continued

**Lemma Statement:**
Let $k$ be a field and $S$ a $k$-algebra. The following are equivalent:

1. "$k' \otimes_k S$ is reduced for every finite purely inseparable extension $k'$ of $k$"

2. "$k^{1/p} \otimes_k S$ is reduced"

3. "$k^{perf} \otimes_k S$ is reduced, where $k^{perf}$ is the perfect closure of $k$"

4. "$\overline{k} \otimes_k S$ is reduced, where $\overline{k}$ is the algebraic closure of $k$"

5. "$S$ is geometrically reduced over $k$"

**Key Definitions from the Text:**
- **Perfect closure:** "$k^{perf}$ is the perfect closure of $k$"
- **Algebraic closure:** "$\overline{k}$ is the algebraic closure of $k$"
- **Purely inseparable extensions:** Referenced as finite extensions of $k$

The page focuses on characterizing when a $k$-algebra maintains the reduced property under base change to various field extensions, with particular emphasis on the relationship between geometric reducedness and tensor product reducedness.

# Lemma 16.8.3: Statement and Proof Outline

## Exact Statement

"Let $R \to A \to \Lambda$ be ring maps with $A$ of finite presentation over $R$. Let $S \subset R$ be a multiplicative set. Let $S^{-1}A \to B' \to S^{-1}\Lambda$ be a factorization with $B'$ smooth over $S^{-1}R$. Then we can find a factorization $A \to B \to \Lambda$ such that some $s \in S$ maps to an elementary standard element in $B$ over $R$."

## Proof Outline

The proof strategy involves:

1. **Standardization step**: Apply Lemma 16.3.4 to assume $B'$ is standard smooth over $S^{-1}R$

2. **Presentation construction**: Express $B'$ as a localization of a polynomial ring with explicit Jacobian invertibility conditions

3. **Clearing denominators**: Clear denominators to obtain a relation involving the determinant of partial derivatives in $R[x_1, \ldots, x_{n+m}]$

4. **Compatibility conditions**: Construct elements $s_j, s'_j \in S$ ensuring relations are satisfied in both the presentation and the target ring $\Lambda$

5. **Final verification**: Show that $s = s_0s_1\ldots s_ts'_1\ldots s'_c$ forms an elementary standard element by verifying the required divisibility relation

I found the information you were looking for. Here are the key results from the Stacks Project on purely inseparable extensions:

**Definition Tag: 09HD**

The main definition is found in Section 9.14: Purely inseparable extensions are the opposite of the separable extensions defined in the previous section. These extensions only show up in positive characteristic.

The definition states: Let $F$ be a field of characteristic $p > 0$. Let $K/F$ be an extension.

**Key Result: Lemma 9.14.6 (Tag 030K)**

An important structural result is that any algebraic field extension is uniquely a separable field extension followed by a purely inseparable one. More specifically, there exists a unique subextension $E/E_{sep}/F$ such that $E_{sep}/F$ is separable and $E/E_{sep}$ is purely inseparable.

# Section 33.6: Geometrically Reduced Schemes

**Section Title:** "33.6 Geometrically reduced schemes"

**Definition (33.6.1):** A scheme $X$ over a field $k$ is called "geometrically reduced at $x$" if for any field extension $k'/k$ and any point $x' \in X_{k'}$ lying over $x$, the local ring $\mathcal{O}_{X_{k'}, x'}$ is reduced. It is "geometrically reduced" over $k$ if this holds at every point.

**Lemmas in this Section:**

- **Lemma 33.6.2:** Local rings of schemes are "geometrically reduced at $x$" iff the ring $\mathcal{O}_{X,x}$ is geometrically reduced over $k$
  
- **Lemma 33.6.3:** Over perfect fields, "if $\mathcal{O}_{X,x}$ is reduced, then $X$ is geometrically reduced at $x$"

- **Lemma 33.6.4:** Multiple equivalent characterizations of geometric reducedness in positive characteristic

- **Lemma 33.6.5:** Point-wise equivalences for geometric reducedness at $x$

- **Lemma 33.6.6:** Geometric reducedness is preserved under base change to field extensions

- **Lemma 33.6.7:** Products with reduced schemes preserve geometric reducedness

- **Lemma 33.6.8:** Geometric reducedness under specialization and for varieties

- **Lemma 33.6.9:** Locally Noetherian schemes have open neighborhoods preserving geometric reducedness

- **Lemma 33.6.11:** Finite purely inseparable extensions achieve geometric reducedness for finitely many components

# Lemma 9.14.6: Decomposition of Algebraic Field Extensions

## Statement

"Let $E/F$ be an algebraic field extension. There exists a unique subextension $E/E_{\text{sep}}/F$ such that $E_{\text{sep}}/F$ is separable and $E/E_{\text{sep}}$ is purely inseparable."

## Proof Outline

The demonstration proceeds by cases based on characteristic:

**Characteristic Zero:** The extension itself is separable, so $E_{\text{sep}} = E$.

**Positive Characteristic $p$:** The proof constructs $E_{\text{sep}}$ as the subextension containing all elements separable over $F$. This forms a field extension by prior results. For any element $\alpha \in E$, a suitable power $q$ of $p$ exists such that $\alpha^q$ becomes separable over $F$, establishing that the remaining extension $E/E_{\text{sep}}$ is purely inseparable. The uniqueness of this decomposition follows directly from these properties.

**Key Result:** Any algebraic extension decomposes uniquely into a separable part followed by a purely inseparable part.

# Section 9.14: Purely Inseparable Extensions

## Definition

**Definition 9.14.1** specifies that for a field $F$ of characteristic $p > 0$ and extension $K/F$:

1. An element $\alpha \in K$ is *purely inseparable* over $F$ if there exists a power $q$ of $p$ such that "$\alpha^q \in F$"

2. The extension $K/F$ is *purely inseparable* if every element of $K$ is purely inseparable over $F$

## Key Characteristics

The section notes that purely inseparable extensions are necessarily algebraic and occur only in positive characteristic. A canonical example involves adjoining $p$th roots: for $t \in F$ without a $p$th root, the field $K = F[t^{1/p}]$ is purely inseparable over $F$.

## Main Lemmas

**Lemma 9.14.2** establishes that "$x^p - t$ is irreducible over $F$" when $t$ lacks a $p$th root.

**Lemma 9.14.6** proves the fundamental decomposition: "There exists a unique subextension $E/E_{sep}/F$ such that $E_{sep}/F$ is separable and $E/E_{sep}$ is purely inseparable."

**Lemma 9.14.8** characterizes the separable degree: "$[K:F]_s = |\text{Mor}_F(K, \overline{F})|$" for finite extensions.

**Lemma 9.14.9** establishes multiplicativity: "$[K:F]_s = [K:E]_s[E:F]_s$" for towers of extensions.

# Lemma 10.140.3 Statement

The exact statement of this lemma concerns the characterization of smooth algebras over fields. Here is the key result:

"The following are equivalent:
1. The $k$-algebra $S$ is smooth at $\mathfrak q$ over $k$.
2. We have $\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \Omega_{S/k} \otimes_S \kappa(\mathfrak q) \leq \dim_x X$.
3. We have $\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \Omega_{S/k} \otimes_S \kappa(\mathfrak q) = \dim_x X$."

Additionally, the lemma establishes that "in this case the local ring $S_{\mathfrak q}$ is regular."

This result provides three equivalent conditions for smoothness of a finite-type $k$-algebra, expressed in terms of the module of differentials and dimension, with the important conclusion that smoothness implies regularity of the localization.

# Section 10.43: Geometrically Reduced Algebras

## Section Title
"10.43 Geometrically reduced algebras"

## Definition
"Let $k$ be a field. Let $S$ be a $k$-algebra. We say $S$ is _geometrically reduced over $k$_ if for every field extension $K/k$ the $K$-algebra $K \otimes_k S$ is reduced." (Definition 10.43.1)

## Lemmas in This Section

**Lemma 10.43.2:** Elementary properties including: subalgebras of geometrically reduced algebras remain geometrically reduced; directed colimits preserve the property; localizations are geometrically reduced.

**Lemma 10.43.3:** Localizations and polynomial rings over geometrically reduced algebras are geometrically reduced.

**Lemma 10.43.4:** Concerning nonreduced behavior, zerodivisors, and idempotents in tensor products—these properties can be detected via finitely generated subalgebras.

**Lemma 10.43.5:** "Let $S$ be a geometrically reduced $k$-algebra. Let $R$ be any reduced $k$-algebra. Then $R \otimes_k S$ is reduced."

**Lemma 10.43.6:** Tensor products with separable or separably generated field extensions preserve reducedness.

**Lemma 10.43.7:** A reduced algebra is geometrically reduced if this property holds at every minimal prime.

**Lemma 10.43.8:** For separable algebraic extensions, the multiplication map admits a localization description.

**Lemma 10.43.9:** Geometric reducedness over $k$ and $k'$ coincide when $k'/k$ is separable algebraic.

# Section 10.42: Separable Extensions

## Section Title
"Separable extensions"

## Definitions

**Definition 10.42.1:** A field extension K/k involves two concepts:

1. K is "separably generated over k" if a transcendence basis exists such that "the extension K/k(x_i; i ∈ I) is a separable algebraic extension."

2. K is "separable over k" if "for every subextension k ⊂ K' ⊂ K with K' finitely generated over k, the extension K'/k is separably generated."

## Lemma Statements

**Lemma 10.42.2:** "Let K/k be a separable field extension. For any subextension K/K'/k the field extension K'/k is separable."

**Lemma 10.42.3:** For a separably generated, finitely generated field extension K/k with r = trdeg_k(K), "there exist elements x₁, …, x_{r + 1} of K" satisfying three conditions about transcendence bases and separability.

**Lemma 10.42.4:** For any finitely generated field extension K/k, "there exists a diagram" with finite purely inseparable extensions k'/k and K'/K such that K'/k' is separably generated.

# Lemma 33.25.7 (056V) - Statement and Proof Outline

## Statement
A reduced scheme X that is locally finite type over a field k is geometrically reduced over k if and only if X contains a dense open subset that is smooth over k.

## Proof Outline

**Forward direction** (dense smooth open ⟹ geometrically reduced):
- Reduces to quasi-compact case locally
- Partitions X into irreducible components X₁, ..., Xₙ
- Notes that a dense smooth open makes X geometrically reduced at generic points of each component (via Lemma 33.25.4)
- Concludes X is geometrically reduced (via Lemma 33.6.8)

**Reverse direction** (geometrically reduced ⟹ dense smooth open):
- Removes intersections of distinct irreducible components (nowhere dense)
- Reduces to irreducible case; such X is integral
- Uses that the function field K of X is a separable extension of k (Algebra Lemma 10.44.2)
- Applies Algebra Lemma 10.140.5 to show some affine localization is smooth over k
- Concludes a dense open of smooth points exists

# Standard Smooth Ring Maps

Based on this lemma, a **standard smooth algebra** is defined as:

> "S = R[x₁, …, xₙ]/(f₁, …, fₓ) = R[x₁, …, xₙ]/I"

where the classes of f₁, …, fₓ modulo (f₁, …, fₓ)² form a basis of a free S-module when projected onto the first c direct summands of the cotangent complex.

## Key Characterizations

The lemma establishes that such algebras satisfy several equivalent properties:

1. The ring map R → S is smooth
2. The module Ω_{S/R} is free with basis dx_{c+1}, …, dx_n
3. The module I/I² is free with basis the classes of f₁, …, fₓ
4. Localizations at elements of S remain standard smooth
5. Base changes along ring maps preserve the standard smooth property
6. Localization of R preserves standard smoothness under invertibility conditions
7. S forms "a relative global complete intersection over R"

The proof leverages the naive cotangent complex structure to establish these equivalences.

# Lemma 30.16.2: Statement and Proof Outline

## Exact Statement

Given a morphism $f : X \to S$ of schemes with a coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ and relatively ample invertible sheaf $\mathcal{L}$, assuming $S$ is Noetherian and $f$ is proper:

"There exists an $n_0$ such that for all $n \geq n_0$ we have $R^pf_*(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}) = 0$ for all $p > 0$."

## Proof Outline

The argument proceeds via reduction to the affine case:

1. **Reduction step**: Cover $S$ with finitely many affine opens; solving the problem locally suffices globally.

2. **Affine case translation**: When $S$ is affine, the vanishing of "higher direct image sheaf equals vanishing of cohomology groups" (Lemma 30.4.6).

3. **Application**: The relative ampleness assumption ensures $\mathcal{L}$ becomes ample on $X$, allowing invocation of the preceding lemma (30.16.1) for the desired cohomology vanishing.

This establishes vanishing of higher cohomology groups for sufficiently large tensor powers.

# Lemma 33.12.3: Geometric Regularity and Field Extensions

## Statement

Let $k$ be a field and $X$ a locally Noetherian scheme over $k$. The lemma establishes that five conditions are equivalent:

1. "$X$ is geometrically regular"
2. "$X_{k'}$ is a regular scheme for every finitely generated field extension $k'/k$"
3. "$X_{k'}$ is a regular scheme for every finite purely inseparable field extension $k'/k$"
4. Every affine open $U \subset X$ has $\mathcal{O}_X(U)$ geometrically regular over $k$
5. An affine open covering exists where each coordinate ring is geometrically regular

## Proof Outline

The proof proceeds through a logical chain:

- **(1) ⟹ (2):** Geometric regularity ensures local rings at all points in $X_{k'}$ are regular for any finitely generated extension, making the entire scheme regular.

- **(2) ⟹ (3):** Finite purely inseparable extensions are a special case of finitely generated extensions.

- **(3) ⟹ (4):** For affine opens, the condition that base changes remain regular for all such extensions translates directly to geometric regularity of coordinate rings.

- **(4) ⟹ (5):** The global property follows from the local one via affine covers.

- **(5) ⟹ (1):** Base changes are obtained by gluing spectra of extended coordinate rings, which remain regular by the geometric regularity assumption.

# Stacks Project — Tag 04QM (Section 33.25, "Schemes smooth over fields")

## Citation
The Stacks Project authors, *The Stacks Project*, Section 33.25 "Schemes smooth over fields" (Part 2, Chapter 33 "Varieties"). Tag `04QM`. <https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/04QM>.

## Slug
stacks-04QM

## Source URL(s)
- <https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/04QM> (section index)
- <https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/056T> (Lemma 33.25.4 — the load-bearing result)
- <https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/056V> (Lemma 33.25.7 — partial converse)

## Why this source

This source underwrites the **(S3.sep.1)** sub-claim of `constants_integral_over_base_field`: a smooth scheme over a field is geometrically reduced. The fact is used in `ChartAlgebra.lean` / `ChartAlgebraS3.lean` to deduce that Γ(X, 𝒪_X) is geometrically reduced over the base field, from which separability of Γ-over-k follows.

## Abstract / Overview

Tag `04QM` is the **section index** for Section 33.25 of the Stacks Project (Part 2, Chapter 33 "Varieties"). It collects ~11 lemmas characterising smooth schemes over a field and translating between smoothness, regularity, geometric regularity, geometric normality, and geometric reducedness.

The directive's seed conflated the section tag with the headline lemma (Lemma 33.25.4, tag `056T`). Both are recorded here.

## Relevant content (per the directive)

### Theorems / Lemmas

- **Lemma 33.25.4** (tag `056T`) — **Smooth implies geometrically regular, normal, reduced over a field.**
  > "Let X → Spec(k) be a smooth morphism where k is a field. Then X is geometrically regular, geometrically normal, and geometrically reduced over k."

  This is the precise statement the project's (S3.sep.1) requires. Its proof in the Stacks Project chains: smooth ⇒ regular fibres ⇒ regular after any base change (since smoothness is preserved under arbitrary base change of the base field, while regularity-of-fibres is preserved), and "geometrically reduced" is implied by "geometrically regular" on a Noetherian scheme.

- **Lemma 33.25.1** — Char-zero criterion: if X/k is locally finite type, Ω_{X/k} locally free, and char(k)=0, then X/k is smooth.

- **Lemma 33.25.2** — Perfect-field criterion: if X/k is locally finite type, Ω_{X/k} locally free, X reduced, and k perfect, then X/k is smooth.

- **Lemma 33.25.3** — A smooth morphism X → Spec(k) makes X a regular scheme.

- **Lemma 33.25.7** (tag `056V`) — **Geometrically reduced ⇔ dense smooth open** (partial converse to 33.25.4):
  > "Let k be a field, let X be a reduced scheme that is locally of finite type over k. Then X is geometrically reduced over k if and only if X contains a dense open which is smooth over k."

  Proof sketch (per Stacks): forward direction via 33.25.4 applied at generic points of irreducible components. Reverse direction uses that the function field is a separable extension of k (Algebra Lemma 10.44.2) plus Algebra Lemma 10.140.5 to find a smooth affine localisation.

- **Lemma 33.25.8** — Over a perfect field k, for a reduced locally algebraic k-scheme X, the smooth locus equals the regular locus and is a dense open subscheme.

- **Lemma 33.25.11** — Any finite-type scheme over a field admits a finite purely inseparable base extension after which it stratifies into smooth locally closed pieces. (Used in route (b) for the (S3.pi.2) step on purely inseparable closures.)

### Cross-references the source relies on

- Algebra Lemma 10.140.7 (char-zero smoothness criterion).
- Algebra Lemma 10.44.2 (separability of function field for geometrically reduced varieties).
- Algebra Lemma 10.140.5 (regular ⇒ smooth at a maximal ideal under suitable conditions).
- Chapter 33 §33.6 (Geometrically reduced schemes — separately summarised at [`stacks-0334.md`](./stacks-0334.md)).

## Caveats

The directive named tag `04QM` for "smooth ⇒ geometrically reduced". That URL resolves to the *section* `33.25`, not to a specific lemma. The exact statement requested is **Lemma 33.25.4 = tag `056T`** (a sublemma of the section). Both tags are valid entry points and both are documented above. No discrepancy with the underlying mathematics, only with the granularity of the tag pointer.

## Quality assessment

Stacks Project is the canonical open-access reference for foundational algebraic geometry; Section 33.25 is the standard citation for the smooth ⇒ geometrically reduced direction. Used as a baseline by Mathlib's `AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Smooth` development.

## Citation
The Stacks Project authors, *The Stacks Project*. <https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/>.

## Slug
stacks-0334

## Source URL(s)
- <https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/0334> — actually resolves to **Proposition 10.162.15** (Algebra, §10.162 "Nagata rings"). Not relevant to geometric reducedness.
- <https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/035U> — **correct tag** for Section 33.6 "Geometrically reduced schemes".
- <https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/05DS> — **correct tag** for Section 10.43 "Geometrically reduced algebras" (the algebra-level definition).
- <https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/030V> — **correct tag** for Lemma 10.44.4 (key characterisation of geometric reducedness via base-change reducedness — see also [`stacks-030V`](./stacks-030V.md) when written).

## Why this source

The directive named tag `0334` as "definition of geometrically reduced". This file documents the **correct** location of that material and flags the mismatch so the planner and writers do not propagate the typo.

## Abstract / Overview

Tag `0334` itself is **Proposition 10.162.15** of the *Algebra* chapter, characterising Nagata rings (equivalence between "Nagata", "every finite-type R-algebra is Nagata", "R is universally Japanese and Noetherian"). This has no direct bearing on the chart-algebra piece (ii) closure.

The actual geometric reducedness material lives in **Section 33.6** (schemes) and **Section 10.43** (algebras).

## Relevant content (per the directive's *intent*, retrieved from the correct tags)

### Definitions

- **Definition 33.6.1** (`035U`, schemes side) — Geometrically reduced at a point / over a field:
  > "A scheme X over a field k is called *geometrically reduced at* x if for any field extension k′/k and any point x′ ∈ X_{k′} lying over x, the local ring 𝒪_{X_{k′}, x′} is reduced. It is *geometrically reduced* over k if this holds at every point."

- **Definition 10.43.1** (`05DS`, algebras side) — Geometrically reduced algebra:
  > "Let k be a field. Let S be a k-algebra. We say S is *geometrically reduced over k* if for every field extension K/k the K-algebra K ⊗_k S is reduced."

### Theorems / Lemmas (Section 33.6 schemes / Section 10.43 algebras)

- **Lemma 33.6.2** — X geometrically reduced at x iff the ring 𝒪_{X,x} is geometrically reduced over k.
- **Lemma 33.6.3** — Over perfect k: reduced 𝒪_{X,x} suffices for geometrically reduced at x.
- **Lemma 33.6.4** — Multiple equivalent characterisations in positive characteristic.
- **Lemma 33.6.5** — Pointwise equivalences for geometrically reduced at x.
- **Lemma 33.6.6** — Preserved under base change to field extensions.
- **Lemma 33.6.7** — Product with a reduced k-scheme preserves geometrically reduced.
- **Lemma 33.6.8** — Behaviour under specialisation and for varieties.
- **Lemma 33.6.9** — Open neighbourhoods preserve geometrically reduced on locally Noetherian schemes.
- **Lemma 33.6.11** — A finite purely inseparable extension of k achieves geometrical reducedness for finitely many components (this is the form used in the (S3.pi.2) step).

- **Lemma 10.43.2** — Subalgebras and directed colimits preserve geometric reducedness; localisations are geometrically reduced.
- **Lemma 10.43.5** — Geometrically reduced ⊗ reduced is reduced (S geometrically reduced, R reduced ⇒ R ⊗_k S reduced).
- **Lemma 10.43.6** — Tensor with separable / separably-generated field extensions preserves reducedness.
- **Lemma 10.43.7** — A reduced algebra is geometrically reduced iff it is geometrically reduced at every minimal prime.
- **Lemma 10.43.9** — Geometric reducedness over k and over k′ coincide when k′/k is separable algebraic.

### Key sister-lemma (tag `030V` = Lemma 10.44.4)

> "Let k be a field, S a k-algebra. The following are equivalent: (1) k′ ⊗_k S is reduced for every finite purely inseparable extension k′/k; (2) k^{1/p} ⊗_k S is reduced; (3) k^{perf} ⊗_k S is reduced; (4) k̄ ⊗_k S is reduced; (5) S is geometrically reduced over k."

This is the workhorse characterisation. Note it actually lives in Section **10.44** (Separable extensions, continued), not 10.43 — Stacks splits the definition (10.43) from the characterisations (10.44).

## Caveats

**Tag `0334` was a typo / misremembered tag.** The dispatcher should cite `035U` (schemes), `05DS` (algebras), or `030V` (the equivalent-characterisations workhorse) instead. The file `stacks-0334.md` is preserved at the directive's requested slug so the dispatch contract is honoured, but anyone reading it should follow the correct cross-references inside.

The correct tags are canonical. Used directly by Mathlib's `RingTheory.GeometricallyReduced` development. Forward reference: **(S3.sep.1)** + **(S3.sep.2)** + **(S3.pi.2)** all draw on this material.

# Stacks Project — Tag 0BJF (Lemma 49.3.1, Discriminant of finite locally free morphism)

## Citation
The Stacks Project authors, *The Stacks Project*, Lemma 49.3.1 ("Discriminant of a finite locally free morphism"), Part 3 Chapter 49 §49.3. Tag `0BJF`. <https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/0BJF>.

## Slug
stacks-0BJF

## Source URL(s)
- <https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/0BJF> — Lemma 49.3.1 (what tag 0BJF actually is).
- Directive intended "geometrically reduced + finite-dim ⇒ separable field extension"; that result is *embedded* in 0BUG part (3)–(4), not at 0BJF. See [`stacks-0BUG.md`](./stacks-0BUG.md).

## Why this source

(S3.sep.2): for a field extension that arises as a connected component of Γ(X, 𝒪_X) where X is proper geometrically reduced over k, deduce separability of the extension over k. The directive named tag `0BJF` but the precise statement actually lives inside Lemma 33.9.3 (tag `0BUG`) part (4).

The tag `0BJF` itself, however, is independently relevant: it gives the trace-pairing characterisation of étale algebras, which is the algebraic input that makes the connected-component-of-Γ pieces *separable* once they are known to be reduced finite k-algebras.

## Abstract / Overview

**Lemma 49.3.1** (tag `0BJF`) lives in Part 3, Chapter 49 (Discriminants and Differents), Section 49.3 (Discriminant of a finite locally free morphism). It is the standard characterisation:

> "Let π : X → Y be a morphism of schemes which is finite locally free. Then π is étale if and only if its discriminant is empty."

## Relevant content (per the directive)

### Theorems / Lemmas

- **Lemma 49.3.1** (tag `0BJF`) — Statement quoted above. **Proof outline** (per Stacks):

  1. **Reduction.** By Morphisms Lemma 29.36.8 it suffices to check fibres are étale. Base change compatibility reduces further: given a field k and a finite-dimensional k-algebra A, show that A is étale over k iff the trace pairing
     $$Q_{A/k}(a, b) := \operatorname{Trace}_{A/k}(ab)$$
     is nondegenerate.

  2. **Forward direction (nondegeneracy ⇒ étale).**
     - Any nilpotent a satisfies Q_{A/k}(a, ·) ≡ 0, so nondegeneracy forces A reduced.
     - A reduced finite k-algebra decomposes as a product of fields A ≅ K_1 × ⋯ × K_n.
     - The trace pairing on A is the orthogonal sum of trace pairings on the K_i.
     - Nondegeneracy of each Tr_{K_i/k} (Fields Lemma 9.20.7) implies each K_i/k is **separable**.
     - Hence A is étale over k (Algebra Lemma 10.143.4).

  3. **Converse.** Reverse the chain: étale ⇒ A reduced product of separable finite extensions ⇒ nondegenerate trace pairing.

  This proof sketch is exactly the algebraic engine the project needs for (S3.sep.2): once Γ(X, 𝒪_X) is known to be a reduced finite k-algebra (by (S3.sep.1) and propriety), its component fields K_i are separable over k iff some equivalent nondegeneracy / unramifiedness condition holds. The cleanest packaging is via 0BUG below.

### The actually-load-bearing statement (lives at 0BUG part 4)

In Lemma 33.9.3 (tag `0BUG`), part (4): if X is **geometrically reduced** and proper over k, then A = Γ(X, 𝒪_X) is a product of fields each of which is a **finite separable extension of k**. This is what the project's (S3.sep.2) actually needs; see [`stacks-0BUG.md`](./stacks-0BUG.md).

The mathematical link: a finite *geometrically* reduced k-algebra (as opposed to merely reduced) automatically has each component field K_i separable over k, because k̄ ⊗_k K_i remains reduced iff K_i/k is separable.

### Cross-references the source relies on

- Morphisms Lemma 29.36.8 (étale on fibres).
- Algebra Lemmas 10.53.2, 10.53.6 (trace pairing in finite locally free setting).
- Algebra Lemma 10.25.1 (idempotent decomposition).
- Algebra Lemma 10.143.4 (étale via separable component fields).
- Fields Lemma 9.20.7 (trace pairing nondegenerate ⇔ separable).

## Caveats

**Tag `0BJF` ≠ the seed claim.** The directive expected `0BJF` to state "geometrically reduced + finite-dim ⇒ separable". What `0BJF` actually proves is the trace-pairing characterisation of étale finite locally free morphisms. The "geometrically reduced finite-dim ⇒ separable" half-step is one *ingredient* of the forward direction (Step 2 above), but it is *not* the lemma's headline statement.

The clean citation for the (S3.sep.2) chain is therefore:

1. **Lemma 33.9.3 part 4** (tag `0BUG`) — geometrically reduced + proper ⇒ A is product of finite separable extensions.
2. **Lemma 49.3.1** (tag `0BJF`) — trace-pairing equivalence, if the proof is restructured around étaleness rather than separability of components.
3. **Fields Lemma 9.20.7** — the direct algebraic input that separable ⇔ nondegenerate trace pairing for finite extensions.

## Quality assessment

Lemma 49.3.1 is the canonical reference for the trace-pairing characterisation. For the project's (S3.sep.2) the *more direct* citation chain is 0BUG → Fields 9.20.7, with 0BJF as supporting infrastructure if the formalisation route goes through étale rather than separable.

# Stacks Project — Tag 02KH (Lemma 30.5.2, Flat base change of higher direct images)

## Citation
The Stacks Project authors, *The Stacks Project*, Lemma 30.5.2 ("Flat base change"), Part 2 Chapter 30 (Cohomology of Schemes) §30.5. Tag `02KH`. <https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/02KH>.

## Slug
stacks-02KH

## Source URL(s)
- <https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/02KH>

## Why this source

This is the workhorse for **(S3.pi.1)** in `ChartAlgebraS3.lean`: for X/k proper and K/k a field extension, the canonical map
$$
\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X) \otimes_k K \longrightarrow \Gamma(X_K, \mathcal{O}_{X_K})
$$
is an isomorphism. The lemma gives the more general statement (any i, any qcqs morphism, any flat base change); we specialise to i = 0 and K a flat k-algebra (every k-algebra is flat over a field).

## Abstract / Overview

Lemma 30.5.2 (tag `02KH`) is the standard "flat base change for higher direct images of quasi-coherent sheaves" lemma in the Stacks Project, applicable as soon as the bottom morphism is qcqs. Its affine specialisation gives the H^i base-change identity that the chart-algebra route needs at i = 0.

## Relevant content (per the directive)

### Theorems / Lemmas

- **Lemma 30.5.2** (tag `02KH`) — **Flat base change.** Consider a Cartesian diagram of schemes:
  ```
       g'
   X' ────► X
   │        │
  f'│       │f
   ▼        ▼
   S' ────► S
       g
  ```
  Let ℱ be a quasi-coherent 𝒪_X-module with pullback ℱ′ = (g′)*ℱ. Assume **g is flat** and **f is quasi-compact and quasi-separated**. Then for every i ≥ 0:

  1. The base-change map
     $$g^* R^i f_* \mathcal{F} \;\longrightarrow\; R^i f'_* \mathcal{F}'$$
     is an isomorphism.

  2. If S = Spec(A) and S′ = Spec(B), then
     $$H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \;=\; H^i(X', \mathcal{F}').$$

- **Specialisation to (S3.pi.1).** Take A = k (a field, hence A → B always flat), B = K a field extension, X/k proper (in particular qcqs), ℱ = 𝒪_X, i = 0. The lemma gives:
  $$\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X) \otimes_k K \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\; \Gamma(X_K, \mathcal{O}_{X_K}).$$
  This is exactly the (S3.pi.1) claim.

### Proof outline (per Stacks)

1. Reduce (1) to (2) by localising to affine base schemes.
2. **Separated case for (2).** Use Čech cohomology with affine covers; show that
   $$\check{C}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) \;=\; \check{C}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B,$$
   and exploit flatness of A → B (tensor commutes with cohomology of a flat complex).
3. **Quasi-separated case.** Apply the Čech-to-cohomology spectral sequence to compare the two situations via flat base change.

### Cross-references the source relies on

- Cohomology Lemma 20.17.1 (definition of the base-change map).
- Morphisms Lemma 29.25.8 (base change preserves flatness).
- Cohomology of Schemes Lemma 30.5.1 (Čech complex base change).
- Cohomology of Schemes Lemmas 30.4.5, 30.4.6 (quasi-coherence of higher direct images for qcqs morphisms).

### Companion: Tag 0BUG / Lemma 33.9.3

For X/k proper one can sharpen the conclusion: Γ(X, 𝒪_X) is a *finite-dimensional* k-algebra; see [`stacks-0BUG.md`](./stacks-0BUG.md). That finite-dimensionality is what subsequently lets (S3.sep.1)+(S3.sep.2) conclude that Γ(X, 𝒪_X) is a finite separable k-algebra when X is geometrically reduced.

## Caveats

The lemma applies to **any qcqs** f, not only proper f. The (S3.pi.1) statement could equally be cited from this general form. The Stacks Project does not use the "Mumford" labelling ("Theorem of Cohomology and Flat Base Change"); the same content in Hartshorne III.9 / Mumford AV §5 carries that name.

Conventions: Stacks works with quasi-coherent (not just coherent) modules in 02KH; the result is purely sheaf-cohomological and does not require properness of f.

## Quality assessment

This is *the* canonical citation for flat base change of cohomology in the modern algebraic-geometry literature. Mathlib's `AlgebraicGeometry.Cohomology.BaseChange` (when it materialises) is expected to follow this proof essentially verbatim.

# Stacks Project — Tag 0BUG (Lemma 33.9.3, Global sections of 𝒪_X on a proper scheme)

## Citation
The Stacks Project authors, *The Stacks Project*, Lemma 33.9.3 ("Properties of A = H^0(X, 𝒪_X) for proper X/k"), Part 2 Chapter 33 (Varieties) §33.9. Tag `0BUG`. <https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/0BUG>.

## Slug
stacks-0BUG

## Source URL(s)
- <https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/0BUG>

## Why this source

**Single most important reference for the (S3.*) chain.** Lemma 33.9.3 packages *all four* (S3.sep.1), (S3.sep.2), (S3.pi.1), (S3.pi.2) sub-claims at once for the special case of A = Γ(X, 𝒪_X), X proper over k. It is the natural ground-truth statement the formalisation should match.

## Abstract / Overview

For X/k proper, Lemma 33.9.3 lists **eight properties** of A := H^0(X, 𝒪_X) — together they form a step-ladder from "finite-dimensional k-algebra" (always true) down through "product of fields" (under reducedness) to "= k" (under geometric integrality), with separability and integrality of the components controlled by geometric reducedness / integrality of X.

## Relevant content (per the directive)

### Theorem

**Lemma 33.9.3** (tag `0BUG`). Let k be a field, X a **proper** scheme over k. Let A = H^0(X, 𝒪_X). Then:

1. **Finiteness.** "A is a finite-dimensional k-algebra."
2. **Local decomposition.** A is a product of *Artinian local* k-algebras, one factor for each connected component of X.
3. **Reduced case.** If X is reduced, then A is a *product of fields*, each a finite extension of k.
4. **Geometrically-reduced case.** If X is geometrically reduced over k, then A is a product of fields, each a finite **separable** extension of k.
5. **Geometrically connected.** If X is geometrically connected, then A ⊗_k k̄ is a 0-dimensional local ring (in particular irreducible).
6. (Follows from 5.) A is a *local* Artinian k-algebra.
7. **Geometrically integral case.** If X is geometrically reduced and geometrically connected — i.e. geometrically integral — then A = k.
8. (Follows from 7.) Same as 7 under the geometrically-integral hypothesis.

### Proof outline (per Stacks)

1. **Finiteness** (1) — Cohomology of Schemes Lemma 30.19.2 (finiteness of H^i for coherent sheaves on a proper k-scheme).
2. **Local Artinian decomposition** (2) — A is a finite-dimensional k-algebra, hence Artinian; idempotents of A correspond to connected components of X (a clopen on X ↔ idempotent in 𝒪_X(X)).
3. **Reducedness ⇒ product of fields** (3) — if X is reduced, then so is A (each local Artinian factor is reduced ⇒ a field).
4. **Geometric reducedness ⇒ separable factors** (4) — base-change A ⊗_k k̄: by Lemma 30.5.2 / flat base change (tag `02KH`), A ⊗_k k̄ = H^0(X_{k̄}, 𝒪_{X_{k̄}}); by hypothesis X_{k̄} is reduced, so A ⊗_k k̄ is reduced; hence each finite extension k ⊂ K_i is **separable** (because k̄ ⊗_k K_i reduced ⇒ K_i/k separable, by Algebra Lemma 10.44.2).
5. **Geometric connectedness ⇒ local A ⊗_k k̄** (5) — connected components of X_{k̄} ↔ idempotents of A ⊗_k k̄; geometric connectedness collapses these to one ⇒ A ⊗_k k̄ has no nontrivial idempotents ⇒ A ⊗_k k̄ is a (0-dimensional, hence local) Artinian local k̄-algebra.
6. (6) Follows from (5) by faithful flatness of k → k̄ (locality descends).
7. (7) Combine (4) and (5): A is a product of separable extensions *and* A ⊗_k k̄ is local; so A is a single finite separable extension of k whose base-change to k̄ is local — which forces A = k.
8. (8) Trivially (7).

### Cross-references the source relies on

- Cohomology of Schemes Lemma 30.19.2 (finiteness of cohomology for proper morphisms on coherent sheaves).
- Lemma 30.5.2 = tag `02KH` (flat base change for higher direct images — see [`stacks-02KH.md`](./stacks-02KH.md)).
- Algebra Lemma 10.44.2 (separability of a finite field extension from reducedness of its tensor with the algebraic closure).
- Algebra §10.43 / §10.44 on geometrically reduced algebras (see [`stacks-0334.md`](./stacks-0334.md), [`stacks-030V.md`](./stacks-030V.md)).

### Where each (S3.*) lands

- **(S3.sep.1)** Smooth ⇒ geometrically reduced: Lemma 33.25.4 (tag `056T`), separate from 0BUG; see [`stacks-04QM.md`](./stacks-04QM.md).
- **(S3.sep.2)** Geometrically-reduced finite k-algebra ⇒ separable factor fields: **Lemma 33.9.3 part (4)**, the headline result.
- **(S3.pi.1)** Γ(X, 𝒪_X) ⊗_k K ≅ Γ(X_K, 𝒪_{X_K}) for X/k proper, K/k arbitrary field extension: invoked inside the proof of part (4) via flat base change; the standalone citation is `02KH`.
- **(S3.pi.2)** Purely inseparable closure: not in 0BUG directly; uses §10.44 / §9.14 (`09HD`, `030K`).

## Caveats

The statement requires **proper**, not merely projective or finite-type. The proper case is what the project needs.

The "geometrically integral ⇒ A = k" half (parts 5–8) is the *Stein factorisation*-style conclusion that the chart-algebra closure of (ii) ultimately drives toward (Γ(X, 𝒪_X) ⊂ k̄ for X smooth projective geometrically integral).

## Quality assessment

This is the central theorem the project's "chart-algebra piece (ii) first-class decomposition" rests on. Every textbook on algebraic geometry over a non-algebraically-closed base field cites some version of this (Hartshorne III.11.5, Mumford GIT Ch.1, Stacks 0BUG). The Stacks form is the most precise: it states all four progressively-refined conclusions in one lemma.

## Citation
The Stacks Project authors, *The Stacks Project*, Lemma 38.7.4 ("Localization and universally injective maps"), Part 2 Chapter 38 (More on Flatness) §38.7. Tag `05DH`. <https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/05DH>.

## Slug
stacks-05DH

## Source URL(s)
- <https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/05DH> — actually Lemma 38.7.4 on universally-injective maps under localisation. **Not about purely inseparable extensions.**
- <https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/09HD> — **correct tag** for Section 9.14 "Purely inseparable extensions" (see [`stacks-09HD.md`](./stacks-09HD.md) below — written as a sibling).
- <https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/030K> — **correct tag** for Lemma 9.14.6, the separable/purely-inseparable decomposition of any algebraic extension.

## Why this source

The directive named tag `05DH` for "purely inseparable extensions". This file documents the mismatch and points to the correct tags.

## Abstract / Overview

Tag `05DH` is **Lemma 38.7.4**, a homological-flatness lemma about when a localised module map is R-universally injective. Its statement:

> "Let R → S be ring maps with S → S′ flat, N a finitely-presented S-module with N flat over R, S of finite presentation over R, and assume that for every associated prime q of N ⊗_R κ(p) (p the inverse image of q in R) the image of Spec(S′) → Spec(S) contains q. Then N → N ⊗_S S′ is R-universally injective."

This has *no direct content* relevant to the chart-algebra piece (ii) closure. It would only enter if the project needed flat-descent of universally-injective maps for purely-formal reasons.

## Relevant content (per the directive's *intent*, retrieved from the correct tags)

### Section 9.14 "Purely inseparable extensions" (tag `09HD`)

**Definition 9.14.1.** Let F be a field of characteristic p > 0, and K/F a field extension.

1. An element α ∈ K is **purely inseparable over F** if there exists a power q of p such that α^q ∈ F.
2. The extension K/F is **purely inseparable** if every element of K is purely inseparable over F.

Note: purely inseparable extensions are automatically algebraic and only exist in positive characteristic. The canonical example is K = F[t^{1/p}] over F, where t ∈ F has no p-th root.

### Key lemmas in §9.14

- **Lemma 9.14.2.** "x^p − t is irreducible over F" when t has no p-th root in F.
- **Lemma 9.14.6** (tag `030K`) — **Separable/purely-inseparable decomposition.** "Let E/F be an algebraic field extension. There exists a unique subextension E/E_{sep}/F such that E_{sep}/F is separable and E/E_{sep} is purely inseparable."
  - Char 0: trivial, E_{sep} = E.
  - Char p > 0: E_{sep} := {α ∈ E : α separable over F}. For any α ∈ E, some power q = p^n satisfies α^q ∈ E_{sep}.
- **Lemma 9.14.8.** [K:F]_s = |Mor_F(K, F̄)| for finite extensions (definition of separable degree).
- **Lemma 9.14.9.** Multiplicativity: [K:F]_s = [K:E]_s · [E:F]_s in towers.

### Relevance to (S3.pi.2)

(S3.pi.2) needs: from a unique-minimal-prime-of-base-change condition, deduce that an extension is purely inseparable. The cleanest packaging:

1. *Unique minimal prime under k̄ base change* ⇔ X_{k̄} (or A ⊗_k k̄) has a unique minimal prime.
2. For a finite reduced k-algebra A = ∏ K_i with all K_i finite over k, A ⊗_k k̄ has a unique minimal prime iff there is a *single* K_i and the extension K_i/k is **purely inseparable** (because the minimal primes of K_i ⊗_k k̄ correspond to k-embeddings K_i ↪ k̄, of which there are [K_i:k]_s; uniqueness forces [K_i:k]_s = 1, i.e. K_i/k purely inseparable).

This is essentially Lemma 9.14.8 applied to a single component.

### Cross-references

- Algebra Section 10.46 (tag `0BR5`) — Universal homeomorphisms; purely inseparable algebraic extensions are exactly universal homeomorphisms on Spec.
- Lemma 33.12.3 (tag `038V`) — Equivalent characterisations of geometrically regular schemes via finite purely inseparable extensions; the same finite-purely-inseparable-extension trick appears in §33.6 (Geometrically reduced) Lemma 33.6.11.
- Lemma 10.45.4 (tag `046W`) — Perfect-closure trick on purely inseparable extensions.

## Caveats

**Tag `05DH` was wrong.** The actual lemma at `05DH` is unrelated to purely inseparable extensions. Cite `09HD` (section), `030K` (decomposition lemma), `0BR5` (universal homeomorphisms), or `038V` (geometric-regularity characterisation) instead, depending on what the formalisation needs.

## Quality assessment

The corrected tags are canonical. Stacks §9.14 is the standard reference for purely inseparable extensions in modern formalised mathematics; Mathlib's `Mathlib.FieldTheory.PurelyInseparable` follows the same definitional choices.

# Stacks Project — Tag 07F4 (DIRECTIVE WAS WRONG — actual content + correct tags)

## Citation
The Stacks Project authors, *The Stacks Project*, Lemma 16.8.3 (Smoothing Ring Maps — "elementary standard" form), Part 1 Chapter 16 (Smoothing Ring Maps) §16.8. Tag `07F4`. <https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/07F4>.

## Slug
stacks-07F4

## Source URL(s)
- <https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/07F4> — Lemma 16.8.3 (elementary-standard-element refinement in the Néron–Popescu smoothing argument). **Not directly about Ω_{S/R} for standard-smooth S/R.**
- <https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/00T7> — **correct tag** for Lemma 10.137.6: standard smooth ring map ⇒ Ω_{S/R} is free with the explicit basis dx_{c+1}, …, dx_n.
- <https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/00T1> — Section 10.137 "Smooth ring maps" (parent section).

## Why this source

(BR.2)–(BR.5) bridge: standard-smooth ⇒ free Ω with explicit basis ⇒ coefficient derivations are well-defined ⇒ `Differential.ContainConstants` holds. The directive named tag `07F4`; the actual statement lives at `00T7`.

## Abstract / Overview

Tag `07F4` is **Lemma 16.8.3**, a technical refinement in Chapter 16 (Smoothing Ring Maps). It states roughly: a smooth factorisation can be promoted to one through an "elementary standard" element. It is part of the Néron–Popescu smoothing machinery, *not* a statement about modules of differentials for standard-smooth algebras.

The directive's intent — "standard smooth ⇒ Ω free" — is correctly stated by **Lemma 10.137.6 = tag `00T7`**.

## Relevant content (per the directive's *intent*)

### Definition (Standard smooth)

A ring map R → S is **standard smooth** of relative dimension n − c if S admits a presentation
$$
S \;=\; R[x_1, \ldots, x_n] / (f_1, \ldots, f_c)
$$
such that the c × c minor of the Jacobian (∂f_i/∂x_j)_{1 ≤ i ≤ c, 1 ≤ j ≤ c} (or some such c-element column choice) is invertible in S. (Tag `00T6` for the precise definition.)

### Theorem (Lemma 10.137.6, tag `00T7`)

For a standard smooth R-algebra S = R[x_1, …, x_n]/(f_1, …, f_c) (presentation as above), the following equivalent properties hold:

1. The ring map R → S is smooth.
2. The S-module **Ω_{S/R} is free with basis dx_{c+1}, …, dx_n**.
3. The S-module I/I² (where I = (f_1, …, f_c)) is free with basis the classes of f_1, …, f_c.
4. Localisations at elements of S remain standard smooth.
5. Base changes along ring maps R → R′ preserve standard smoothness.
6. (Localisation of R under invertibility) preserves standard smoothness.
7. S is a relative global complete intersection over R.

The proof leverages the naive cotangent complex: the short exact sequence
$$
0 \to I/I^2 \to \Omega_{R[x_\bullet]/R} \otimes_{R[x_\bullet]} S \to \Omega_{S/R} \to 0
$$
splits because the Jacobian condition exhibits the first c basis vectors of the middle term as a basis of the kernel; the remaining n − c basis vectors descend to Ω_{S/R}.

### Coefficient derivations and ContainConstants

For standard smooth S/R with Ω_{S/R} free on dx_{c+1}, …, dx_n, the dual basis gives **coefficient derivations** ∂_{c+1}, …, ∂_n ∈ Der_R(S, S), with ∂_j(x_i) = δ_{ij}. These are exactly the derivations needed to define `Differential.ContainConstants` in the Lean project: a subring T ⊂ S "contains constants" iff every ∂_j (j > c) vanishes on T iff T ⊂ image-of-R-as-coefficients.

This conceptual picture is in **Stacks Section 10.131** (tag `00RM`, Differentials) and **Section 17.28** (tag `08RL`, Modules of differentials) — not in tag `07F4`.

### Cross-references

- Lemma 10.136.9 (tag `00SS`) — Naive cotangent complex.
- Lemma 10.137.4 (tag `00T5`) — Local complete intersection presentation.
- Lemma 10.137.9 (tag `00TT`) — Smooth at a maximal ideal ⇒ standard smooth in a Zariski neighbourhood (specifically: there exists g ∉ 𝔪 such that S_g is standard smooth over R).
- Section 10.140 (tag `00TQ`) — Smooth algebras over fields (gives Ω locally free + correct rank).

## Caveats

**Tag `07F4` is misattributed.** Use `00T7` for the headline statement, `00T6` for the definition, and `00TT` for the "smooth localises to standard smooth" sharpening.

The directive also referenced "tag 07F1" for the cotangent module of standard smooth algebras. That tag was not separately fetched, but Section 10.137 entries near `00T6`–`00T9` cover this same ground; `07F1` is in Chapter 16 (Smoothing Ring Maps) — likely also a smoothing-step lemma rather than the headline standard-smooth Ω statement.

## Quality assessment

Lemmas 10.137.4–10.137.9 (`00T5`–`00TT`) form the canonical reference chain for standard smoothness and its consequences on differentials. Mathlib's `RingTheory.Smooth.StandardSmooth` (when developed) follows these same lemmas. The corrected reference is the load-bearing one for the (BR.2)–(BR.5) bridge.

- **FGA original (Grothendieck).** Alexandre Grothendieck, *Fondements de la Géométrie Algébrique*, Séminaire Bourbaki Exposés **190** (1959/60), **195** (1959/60), **212** (1960/61), **221** (1960/61), **232** (1961/62). Available openly via Numdam.
- **FGA Explained.** *Fundamental Algebraic Geometry: Grothendieck's FGA Explained*, eds. B. Fantechi, L. Göttsche, L. Illusie, S. Kleiman, N. Nitsure, A. Vistoli. AMS Mathematical Surveys and Monographs **123** (2005). ISBN 978-0-8218-3541-8. The Kleiman and Nitsure chapters are also available as standalone arXiv preprints.

## Slug

## Source URL(s)

- **Nitsure, "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes"** (FGA Explained Chapter 5, pp. 105–137):
  - arXiv:math/0504590 — <https://arxiv.org/abs/math/0504590>
- **Original Grothendieck Bourbaki seminars (Numdam, open):**

  - Exp. 195: <http://www.numdam.org/item/SB_1959-1960__5__369_0/>

## Why this source

For STRATEGY.md grounding of **Route A (FGA-style Picard scheme construction)**, even though Route A is off the iter-150 critical path. The dispatcher wants this in `references/` so future iterations rediscover the right open-access entry points rather than re-rediscovering them.

## Abstract / Overview

The Picard scheme — and the Hilbert / Quot scheme machinery underlying it — was sketched by Grothendieck in five Bourbaki talks (FGA, 1959/60–61/62) and given expanded treatments in Kleiman's "The Picard scheme" and Nitsure's "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes" (both in FGA Explained, 2005). The Picard scheme Pic_{X/S} is the relative-moduli scheme classifying isomorphism classes of invertible sheaves on a family X → S; its construction proceeds via the Quot scheme and uses cohomology-and-base-change, properness, and projectivity hypotheses on X/S.

## Relevant content (per the directive — shallow)

### Kleiman, *The Picard scheme* (arXiv:math/0504020; FGA Explained Ch. 9)

- **Length / scope.** ~83 pages of expanded ICTP-2003 lecture notes. Combines a rigorous development of the Picard scheme with a historical introduction (Bernoulli → Grothendieck).
- **Primary classification.** Algebraic Geometry, MSC 14K30 (abelian varieties and the Picard scheme). Secondary 14C20, 14C17 (classical algebraic geometry) and 14-03 (history).
- **Coverage.** Picard functor; Picard scheme existence theorems (Grothendieck, Murre, Artin); separated / proper / projective hypotheses on X/S; representability via Quot scheme; tangent space (= H^1(X, 𝒪_X)); local structure.
- **Key dependencies the paper relies on:** Hilbert/Quot schemes (Nitsure, FGA Explained Ch. 5); flat base change of cohomology; semicontinuity (Stacks `02KH`, Hartshorne III.12); the Theorem on Formal Functions.

### Nitsure, *Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes* (arXiv:math/0504590; FGA Explained Ch. 5)

- **Length / scope.** 36 pages, ICTP-2003 lectures. Expository account of Grothendieck's construction of Hilbert and Quot schemes, with additional refinements by Mumford and Altman–Kleiman.
- **Coverage.** Quot_{ℱ/X/S}^{P, L}: definitions; representability theorem (Grothendieck): for X/S projective with relatively ample L, the functor Quot_{ℱ/X/S}^{P, L} is representable by a projective S-scheme; flattening stratifications; Castelnuovo–Mumford regularity input.
- **Why the project may eventually need this.** Pic_{X/S} (Route A) is built from Quot in Kleiman's chapter; if the formalisation ever pursues that route, Nitsure's exposition is the natural Lean target.

### Caveats — original Grothendieck Bourbaki seminars

The five Bourbaki *exposés* (190, 195, 212, 221, 232) are openly accessible via Numdam but written in dense original style and assume substantial background in EGA. **For formalisation purposes, the Kleiman and Nitsure expositions are the load-bearing references**; the original Bourbaki talks are for historical and conceptual grounding only.

## Caveats

- **Verification depth:** abstract and scope of Kleiman and Nitsure papers verified via arXiv abstract pages. Full sectional table-of-contents of either paper was not extracted from arXiv (the PDF was binary; the abstract page does not display ToC). Future writers wanting to cite specific theorems must open the PDFs.
- **Numdam URLs** for the original Bourbaki seminars are listed by standard indexing but not directly fetched in this dispatch. They should be considered indicative; the canonical access is through <https://www.numdam.org> with a search for "Grothendieck" + "Technique de descente" + year.
- **Route A is off the iter-150 critical path.** The chart-algebra piece (ii) closure (the actual iter-150 driver) does *not* need Picard scheme machinery; Stacks tags `04QM`, `0BUG`, `02KH`, `00T7` are what backs it.

## Quality assessment

- **Kleiman 2005 / 2014** is *the* definitive modern English-language exposition of the Picard scheme. Canonical citation.
- **Nitsure 2005** is *the* definitive modern English-language exposition of Hilbert and Quot schemes. Canonical citation.
- **FGA Explained** (book) is the standard reference for Grothendieck's expository style; the book itself is paywalled (AMS) but the chapters by Kleiman and Nitsure are openly available as arXiv preprints.
- The original FGA Bourbaki seminars (Numdam, open) are valuable for citations but rarely the right pedagogical pointer for new work.
